en.extract:
The marigold lorikeet or Sumba lorikeet (Trichoglossus capistratus) is a species of parrot that is endemic to the Southeast Asian islands of Sumba, Rote, Wetar and Kisar (Indonesia) and Timor (Indonesia and Timor-Leste). It was previously considered a subspecies of the rainbow lorikeet, but following a review in 1997, it is increasingly treated as a separate species.
Overall, the marigold lorikeet resembles a faded rainbow or scarlet-breasted lorikeet, with the blue to the head not as strongly demarcated and the chest ranging from orange-yellow to yellow. In addition to the nominate subspecies from Timor, it includes the subspecies fortis ("Edward's lorikeet") from Sumba and flavotectus ("Wetar lorikeet") from Wetar. It inhabits the edge of primary forest, secondary forest, woodland and plantations at altitudes below 500 m (1,600 ft) on Timor, but at least up to 950 m (3,120 ft) on Sumba.  It remains fairly common, but its relatively small distribution could give cause for future concern.


References


External links

Marigold Lorikeet. Oriental Bird Images.

The '''marigold lorikeet''' or Sumba lorikeet ('''''Trichoglossus capistratus''''')<ref>Lexicon of Parrots</ref> is a species of [[parrot]] that is [[Endemism|endemic]] to the [[Southeast Asia|Southeast Asian]] islands of [[Sumba]], [[Rote Island|Rote]], [[Wetar]] and [[Kisar]] (Indonesia) and [[Timor]] (Indonesia and [[Timor-Leste]]). It was previously considered a [[subspecies]] of the [[rainbow lorikeet]],<ref name=JuniperParr>Juniper, T., & M. Parr (1998). ''A Guide to the Parrots of the World.'' Pica Press. {{ISBN|1-873403-40-2}}</ref> but following a review in 1997,<ref>[[Richard Schodde|Schodde, R.]] & [[Ian J. Mason|I. J. Mason]] (1997). ''Zoological Catalogue of Australia, Volume 37, Part 2: Aves (Columbidae to Coraciidae).'' Australian Biological Resources Study. {{ISBN|0-643-06037-5}}</ref> it is increasingly treated as a separate species.<ref name=H&M>Dickinson, E. C. (editor) (2003). ''The Howard and Moore Complete Checklist of the Birds of the World.'' 3d edition. Christopher Helm. {{ISBN|0-7136-6536-X}}</ref><ref>Gill, F., M. Wright, & D. Donsker (2009). ''[http://www.worldbirdnames.org/ IOC World Bird Names].'' Version 2.1. Accessed 20-06-2009</ref>

Overall, the marigold lorikeet resembles a faded rainbow or [[scarlet-breasted lorikeet]], with the blue to the head not as strongly demarcated and the chest ranging from orange-yellow to yellow.<ref name=JuniperParr/> In addition to the [[nominate subspecies]] from Timor, it includes the subspecies ''fortis'' ("Edward's lorikeet") from Sumba and ''flavotectus'' ("Wetar lorikeet") from Wetar.<ref name=H&M/> It inhabits the edge of [[primary forest]], [[secondary forest]], woodland and plantations at altitudes below {{convert|500|m|ft|abbr=on}} on Timor, but at least up to {{convert|950|m|ft|abbr=on}} on Sumba.<ref name= Wallacea>Coates, B. J., & K. D. Bishop (1997). ''A Guide to the Birds of Wallacea.'' Dove Publications Pty. Ltd. {{ISBN|0-9590257-3-1}}</ref>  It remains fairly common,<ref name= Wallacea/> but its relatively small distribution could give cause for future concern.
